title: Krylov Complexity: Flat bands and Carroll breaking deformations
authors: Aritra Banerjee, Arpan Bhattacharyya, Rudranil Basu, Sayan Das
abstract: Systems with flat band structures, when written in the language of Compact Localised States (CLS), have been shown to be explicitly invariant under supertranslation symmetries, making Carrollian symmetries inherently important for such systems. In this work, we explore the state dynamics of these systems, focusing on quenches induced by Carroll breaking perturbations, through the probe of Krylov (spread) Complexity. We specialise to Fermionic ladder Hamiltonians with all bands flat (ABF) scenario, augmented by a supertranslation preserving interaction, and discuss Krylov state complexity for quenches across critical lines. We further discuss how the growth of Krylov complexity sharply resolves the phase-dependent resilience of Carrollian sectors against delocalising perturbations. This is augmented by a complementary mechanism for Krylov growth in a continuum Carroll scalar field theory with a gradient deformation, which exhibits strong ultraviolet sensitivity (UV/IR mixing).
summary: The paper uses Krylov Complexity as a quantitative measure to probe the stability of Carrollian symmetries in flat band fermionic models. By analyzing quenches across different phases, it demonstrates that this complexity growth reveals how robustly protected these exotic symmetries are against external perturbations. This provides deep insight into non-equilibrium dynamics in strongly correlated materials.
main_problem: The study investigates how state dynamics in flat band systems, characterized by emergent Carrollian symmetries, evolve when subjected to perturbations that break these symmetries.
main_result: Krylov complexity proves to be a powerful diagnostic tool, quantitatively measuring the resilience and destruction of Carroll symmetry across different initial phases (Vanilla vs. Exotic).
method: The analysis employs Krylov Complexity ($C_K(t)$) by projecting time evolution onto a dynamically generated orthonormal basis derived from the Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The primary model is an interacting fermionic ladder Hamiltonian exhibiting flat bands, which are augmented by Carroll-breaking perturbations. A continuum scalar field theory with gradient deformation is also analyzed.
key_observables: Krylov Complexity ($C_K(t)$), Krylov Inverse Participation Ratio (KIPR), real-space spreading widths of excitons, and the initial growth rate coefficient $b_2^1$.
important_parameters: The coupling strength ($\lambda$), lattice parameters ($	au$, $V$), and the quench protocol defining the initial state (Vanilla vs. Exotic phase).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on short-time approximations for complexity growth, and in continuum limits, it assumes a double-scaling prescription to handle UV/IR mixing.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams defining different quantum phases (Vanilla/Exotic) and show the time evolution of Krylov complexity or real-space spreading profiles under perturbations.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical framework using flat bands and Carroll symmetry, then applying Krylov complexity to analyze quenches across critical lines in lattice models, and finally extending the diagnostic tool to continuum field theories exhibiting UV/IR mixing.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses non-equilibrium dynamics and spectral properties in strongly correlated electron systems (flat band physics) using advanced quantum diagnostics like Krylov complexity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The phase plot corresponding to the Hamiltonian (10). Regions I, II, and III are, respectively, the Vanilla-β, Vanilla-α, and Exotic phases. The discussion in the main text focuses on the τ > 0 side, where the Vanilla-β phase meets the Exotic phase at V = −4τ.

FIG. 2: Real-space propagation of the soft half-filled exciton near the Carroll critical line. The calculation is performed in the projected one-exciton subspace |m, n⟩= α† mβn|Ψβ⟩for L = 40, τ = 1, ∆= 0.03, and V = −4.01. The soft off-site exciton has energy scale 4τ + V, while the deformation H∆supplies a finite propagation velocity. The heat map in panel (a) shows the ballistic spreading of the β hole, and panel (b) shows the corresponding growth of the second moment. This provides a real-space diagnostic of the same Carroll-breaking mechanism that later drives Krylov-space spreading.

FIG. 3: Krylov complexity growth for L = 16 rungs, i.e. 32 ladder sites. ∆= 0.01. For the Vanilla-reference all-β quench we use V = −4τ−ϵ, while for the Exotic-reference doublon/empty quenches we use V = −4τ+ϵ. We chose τ = 1 and ϵ = 10−4. The Lanczos calculation is performed in the exact fixed-(No, Ne) parity block described in Appendix D.

FIG. 4: Early time KIPR for the four representative states. Other physical parameters are same as Figure (3). For the Vanilla quench, the KIPR saturates at a comparatively lower value, indicating strong spreading in Krylov space. By contrast, the P4 state saturation value goes even lower, consistent with the discussion we have had in the text. DW states as usual take a middle path.

FIG. 5: Finite-time Krylov response in the (ϵ, ∆) plane at t⋆= 10, with V = −4τ + ϵ and τ = 1. The three panels show K(t⋆; ϵ, ∆) for the all-β-filled state, the doublon domain-wall state, and the P4 doublon pattern, respectively, using a common color scale. The CDW state is not shown because it is an exact frozen state for all (ϵ, ∆), with KCDW(t) = 0. The maps display the expected hierarchy of dynamical vulnerability: the P4 and all-β states show the strongest response, while the domain wall remains much less susceptible to the ∆-quench.
